How to find it: When you have a forcing move available, do not automatically take the obvious capture. At this level, compare forcing moves by their follow-up value, not just by whether they win material. Takeaway: Before choosing a natural capture, check whether a forcing move creates a second target and keeps the initiative.

Rb1. Your Re1 was a very reasonable consolidating move because it defends the queen on d1 and the pawn on e4, but it is a bit too single-purpose. Rb1 improves the rook while doing two useful jobs at once: it attacks the pawn on b7 and defends the bishop on c1. That is the more instructive choice because it combines activity with prophylaxis — you create pressure on a target while also making sure your own bishop is not left as a loose tactical detail. How to find it: In a quiet position with no undefended pieces, do not stop at the first useful defensive move. Run a two-job move check: first ask, 'Which of my pieces or pawns can I defend?' then ask, 'Which enemy pawn or square can I pressure?' Prefer the move that answers both questions. Here, Re1 only defends, while Rb1 defends the bishop on c1 and attacks the pawn on b7, so it is the more efficient practical move. Takeaway: When there is no immediate tactic, look for a move that improves a piece while also defending something and creating pressure.

I'd play bxc4. Your move was a sensible beginner choice: the pawn on b5 was attacked, and bxc4 uses that pawn to capture White's pawn on c4. The key idea is simple: when one of your pawns is under pressure, look for a capture that solves the problem instead of just moving away. After bxc4, your pawn has taken a central pawn and attacks the pawn on d3. Takeaway: When an attacked pawn can capture something, check that capture before making a passive move.

I'd play g6. Your bxc4 is a very natural move because it captures the pawn on c4 and attacks d3, but it commits the b-pawn immediately. g6 is the more instructive move because it improves your position without releasing the tension: you keep the c4 pawn as a target and preserve flexibility for the next move. A simple follow-up idea is that after g6, if White plays Bc3, Rg8 can defend the bishop on f8 and the pawn on g7. Takeaway: When a capture is available, first ask whether you can improve with tempo or keep the tension before committing your pawn structure.

Qd7 improves the queen while defending both b5 and c7, so it stabilizes your queenside structure without committing a pawn yet. The key idea is prophylaxis: before starting a pawn plan, make sure your important pawns are covered so White cannot gain time by attacking them. How to find it: In a quiet position with no loose pieces, run this advanced checklist: first ask, 'What pawn breaks or pawn moves are available to me?' Then ask, 'Which pawns would I want to keep stable before committing?' Here, b5 and c7 are the pawns that matter, so look for a move that defends both while improving a piece.
